skill.name: drugbank-database
skill.description: Access DrugBank data for pharmaceutical research, including drug properties, interactions, targets, pathways, chemical structures, and pharmacology workflows.
skill.summary: Query and analyze DrugBank drug, interaction, target, pathway, and chemical structure data.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed malicious behavior or prompt injection. Most static findings are false positives from markdown code fences, XML examples, chemistry terms such as SMILES and ADME, PCA variable names, and legitimate DrugBank examples. Residual risk is medium because the skill documents credential handling, authenticated network downloads, local credential/config files, local caches, and a helper script that loads external DrugBank data.

medium_findings.title: Credential and API Key Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to configure DrugBank usernames, passwords, and API authorization headers. This is legitimate for DrugBank access, but mishandling these values could expose credentials in shell history, config files, notebooks, or shared scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential flows are explicit and semantically tied to DrugBank authentication. They are legitimate, but they create a real handling risk for users.

medium_findings.title: Network Download and API Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill documents authenticated downloads and REST API requests to DrugBank. This is expected functionality, but publication should disclose that use may contact external services and depends on DrugBank license compliance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is clearly documented and points to the advertised data source. No exfiltration intent was found, but external authenticated access is a real operational risk factor.

medium_findings.title: Local Credential and Cache Files
medium_findings.description: The references show a plaintext local configuration file and local data/cache locations under the user home directory. These are normal for data tooling, but users should protect permissions and avoid storing secrets in shared environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file locations and cache examples are directly present in the documentation. The risk is moderate because the paths are local and expected, not hidden exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown code fences and inline code examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They do not execute when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are fenced documentation blocks. There is no Ruby code path or dynamic command execution at those locations.

low_findings.title: Security Keyword False Positives in Scientific Terms
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography and C2 detections are caused by scientific or analytic terms such as SMILES, ADME, descriptions, and PCA component names. No cryptographic implementation or command-and-control behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear as chemistry fields, drug descriptions, or PCA labels. The surrounding context does not implement crypto or remote control behavior.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance False Positives
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings correspond to XML traversal, local file existence checks, and graph statistics in research examples. They do not enumerate host users, processes, networks, or system secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines operate on DrugBank XML or analysis objects. I did not find evidence of host reconnaissance intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated External Data Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill intentionally supports DrugBank downloads and API calls. Users should expect outbound requests when running the documented downloader or API examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real but aligned with the advertised purpose. The main concern is transparency, credential protection, and license compliance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Writes for Research Outputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples write local caches and exported analysis files. This can overwrite files if users reuse names, but the paths are ordinary local outputs and not stealth persistence.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are explicit cache or export examples. They are not hidden, but they still warrant disclosure as filesystem behavior.

content.value_statement: Drug discovery and pharmacology work often requires structured access to complex DrugBank records. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical workflows for querying drugs, interactions, targets, pathways, and chemical properties.
actual_capabilities: Guides DrugBank account setup, credential configuration, downloads, version selection, and local XML parsing.
Shows how to query drugs by DrugBank ID, name, CAS number, keyword, and drug type.
Extracts drug descriptions, indications, identifiers, pharmacology fields, chemical properties, SMILES, InChI, and cross references.
Analyzes drug-drug interactions, bidirectional interaction networks, severity categories, and polypharmacy risk scores.
Maps drug targets, enzymes, transporters, carriers, pathways, UniProt identifiers, and Gene Ontology terms.
Supports structure-based workflows such as fingerprints, similarity search, Lipinski checks, clustering, and chemical property export.
limitations: Requires valid DrugBank access and compliance with DrugBank license terms.
Most examples depend on external Python packages such as drugbank-downloader, pandas, RDKit, NetworkX, and scikit-learn.
Interaction classifications and ADMET predictions are screening aids, not clinical decisions or medical advice.
Large DrugBank XML files may require local storage, memory planning, and caching for reliable performance.

use_cases.title: Build Drug Research Datasets
use_cases.description: Create reproducible DrugBank-derived tables with identifiers, indications, mechanisms, chemical properties, and external database links.

use_cases.title: Review Polypharmacy Risks
use_cases.description: Check a drug list for known interactions, summarize mechanisms, and flag combinations that need expert review.

use_cases.title: Explore Repurposing Candidates
use_cases.description: Compare targets, pathways, chemical similarity, and known indications to prioritize drug repurposing hypotheses.

prompt_templates.title: Find One Drug Record
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the DrugBank skill to look up DrugBank ID DB00001. Summarize its name, type, indication, mechanism of action, and key identifiers.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Drug Interactions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the DrugBank interaction workflow to compare DB00001, DB00002, and DB00005. Report known pairwise interactions and explain the likely mechanisms.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Targets and Pathways
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the targets and pathways references to extract targets, enzymes, transporters, pathway links, and UniProt IDs for aspirin. Organize the results by biological role.

prompt_templates.title: Run Similarity and ADMET Screening
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chemical analysis workflow to find structurally similar approved drugs for a candidate SMILES string. Include fingerprints, similarity threshold, Lipinski checks, and limitations.

output_examples.input: Check a three-drug regimen for known DrugBank interactions.
output_examples.output: A concise interaction summary listing each drug pair, whether an interaction is known, the DrugBank description, likely severity category, and follow-up review notes.

output_examples.input: Summarize target data for one DrugBank compound.
output_examples.output: A target profile with protein names, actions, known-action status, UniProt identifiers, metabolic enzymes, transporters, carriers, and pathway context.

output_examples.input: Prepare a chemical similarity screen for approved drugs.
output_examples.output: A workflow summary covering structure extraction, fingerprint choice, Tanimoto threshold, property filters, candidate ranking, and validation warnings.

best_practices: Use environment variables for DrugBank credentials and keep API keys out of notebooks and shared files.
Pin the DrugBank database version and record it in every analysis output.
Treat interaction, similarity, and ADMET outputs as research evidence that needs domain expert review.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode DrugBank usernames, passwords, or API keys in scripts.
Do not use generated interaction scores as direct clinical guidance.
Do not mix DrugBank versions inside one analysis without documenting the change.

faq.question: Does this skill include the DrugBank database?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows for accessing and analyzing DrugBank data after the user obtains licensed access.

faq.question: Can it download DrugBank automatically?
faq.answer: It documents use of drugbank-downloader, which can download data when valid credentials and network access are available.

faq.question: Can this replace clinical decision support software?
faq.answer: No. Interaction analysis is for research and triage. Clinical decisions require qualified review and validated systems.

faq.question: Which data formats does it cover?
faq.answer: The references focus on XML parsing, with notes for API JSON, CSV or TSV exports, SQL dumps, and pandas DataFrames.

faq.question: Does it support chemical similarity analysis?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes SMILES and InChI extraction, RDKit fingerprints, Tanimoto similarity, substructure search, and clustering workflows.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The generated metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
